segments.caption: The video begins with a black screen displaying the title 'EPIC HISTORY TV' in bold red and white text, followed by the subtitle 'THE HISTORY OF RUSSIA: RURIK TO REVOLUTION' in white. The scene transitions to a rotating globe, zooming in on the Eurasian continent, highlighting the region of Russia and Ukraine. The timeline progresses backward, starting from 3975 BC, showing ancient human settlements and skeletal remains emerging on the map. By 2000 BC, images of burial mounds and archaeological findings appear, indicating early human habitation. The timeline advances to 450 BC, introducing 'THE SCYTHIANS' with a bust of Herodotus labeled 'FATHER OF HISTORY' appearing on the left. The map then shifts to 345 AD, showing the movement of 'THE HUNS' and 'THE GOTHS' toward the Roman Empire, marked by red arrows. By 550 AD, 'THE AVARS' are introduced, followed by 650 AD, where the map displays the division of Slavic tribes into 'THE WEST SLAVS', 'THE EAST SLAVS', and 'THE SOUTH SLAVS', with further subdivisions like 'ILMEN SLOVENIANS', 'KRIVICHI', 'VYATIHI', 'SEVERIANS', 'POLANS', 'ULICHS', 'TIVERZI', 'POMERANIANS', 'VELETI', 'VISTULANS', 'CZECHS', 'CROATS', 'SERBS', and 'DRAGUVITY' appearing across the region. The visual narrative emphasizes the historical evolution and migration patterns of ancient peoples across Eastern Europe and Russia, using animated maps, text overlays, and archaeological imagery to illustrate key periods and cultural groups.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a historical map of Eastern Europe in 650 AD, showing the distribution of various Slavic tribes such as the Ilmen Slovenians, Krivichi, Vyatichi, Severians, Polans, Ulichs, Tiverzi, Pomeranians, Veleti, Vistulans, Czechs, Croats, Serbs, and Draguvity. As the timeline advances to 800 AD, the map highlights major rivers like the Dnieper, Don, and Volga, with an illustration of 'THE VARANGIANS' appearing on the left, depicting Norse warriors in armor and a longship. By 862 AD, the focus shifts to Prince Rurik, whose portrait is displayed with the text 'PRINCE RURIK 862–879', and the city of Novgorod is marked on the map. The 'RURIKID DYNASTY 862–1598' is introduced with a decorative banner, and the region is labeled 'LAND OF THE RUS'. In 882 AD, Prince Oleg (879–912) is introduced, and the capital shifts to Kiev, with the territory now called 'KIEVAN RUS'. By 988 AD, the map features Vladimir the Great (980–1015), and a statue of him holding a Christian cross appears on the right, symbolizing the Christianization of Kievan Rus. The timeline and visual elements collectively narrate the rise of the Rurikid Dynasty and the formation of the Kievan Rus state, culminating in its Christianization.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 988 AD, focusing on Kievan Rus with Kiev marked at the confluence of the Dnieper River. The map highlights key rulers: Prince Sviatoslav I (962?–972), Prince Yaropolk I (972–980), and Vladimir the Great (980–1015), whose portrait appears alongside a stone statue depicting him holding a Christian cross, symbolizing the Christianization of Kievan Rus. As time progresses to 1015 AD, Vladimir the Great’s portrait remains, and Prince Sviatopolk (1015–1019) is introduced. By 1040 AD, the map expands to show surrounding powers like the Holy Roman Empire, Kingdom of Poland, Kingdom of Hungary, Byzantine Empire, and the Abbasid Caliphate, with Yaroslav the Wise (1019–1054) now featured. The map then zooms into Kievan Rus, showing its fragmentation into principalities like Novgorod, Polotsk, Chernigov, and Halych by 1054 AD. In 1205 AD, the map shows further division into regions such as Rostov-Suzdal, Smolensk, and Murom-Ryazan. The narrative shifts dramatically in 1223 AD with the appearance of the Mongol Empire, marked in red, and a portrait of Genghis Khan. A red dotted line traces the Mongol advance westward, culminating in the Battle of the Kalka River in 1223, illustrated with a banner and crossed swords. By 1237 AD, the Mongol invasion intensifies, with red smoke engulfing cities like Rostov, Vladimir, Ryazan, and Kiev, and a medieval illustration of a siege appears on the right, depicting soldiers attacking a fortified town with flames and chaos.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 1237 AD, showing a map of Eastern Europe with cities such as Kiev, Rostov, Vladimir, Ryazan, and Halych engulfed in red flames, symbolizing the Mongol invasion. A medieval siege illustration on the right depicts soldiers attacking a fortified town with chaos and fire, representing the destruction. The timeline advances to 1242 AD, shifting focus to Novgorod, where the Battle of the Ice is highlighted with a banner and crossed swords, opposing the Teutonic Knights. An icon of Alexander Nevsky appears, depicted in traditional Russian religious art style with a halo, golden robe, and dark cloak, emphasizing his role as a defender. The map then zooms out to show the vast territory of the Golden Horde, marked in orange, stretching from the Volga to the Black Sea, with Sarai labeled as its capital. By 1253 AD, the map displays the fragmented principalities of Kievan Rus—Republic of Novgorod, Grand Principality of Vladimir, Pskov, Polotsk, Smolensk, Kiev, and Galicia-Volhynia—now under the shadow of the Golden Horde. The phrase 'The Tatar Yoke' appears on a scroll, symbolizing subjugation. In 1283 AD, Moscow emerges as a new political center, marked on the map, indicating its rising importance under Mongol rule. The atmosphere is dark and dramatic, with smoky textures and fiery reds, emphasizing conquest and domination.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a historical map of Eastern Europe in 1283 AD, highlighting the dominance of the Golden Horde over much of the region, marked in orange, with the label 'THE TATAR YOKE' at the bottom. Key political entities such as the Republic of Novgorod, Grand Principality of Vladimir, and cities like Moscow, Kiev, and Smolensk are labeled. As the timeline advances to 1326 AD, the map shows the gradual expansion of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania in green, encroaching on territories previously under the Golden Horde. By 1362 AD, the Grand Duchy of Lithuania has expanded further, and a new banner appears: 'THE BLUE WATERS 1362', accompanied by crossed swords, indicating a significant military or political event. The focus then shifts to 1380 AD, with a portrait of Dmitri Donskoi appearing near Moscow, and a banner reading 'KULIKOVO FIELD 1380' with crossed swords, marking the famous battle against the Mongols. A brief animated battle scene follows, depicting mounted warriors in colorful attire clashing in a chaotic melee. The timeline progresses to 1420 AD, showing the Grand Principality of Moscow expanding, while the Golden Horde begins to fragment. By 1447 AD, the Golden Horde has broken into successor states: the Khanate of Kazan, Nogai Horde, Khanate of Astrakhan, and others, with the map now showing the Sibir Khanate and Kazakh Khanate. In 1453 AD, the Ottoman Empire appears in deep red, expanding into the Balkans and capturing Constantinople, while the Crimean Khanate is labeled near the Black Sea. The map remains focused on the evolving political landscape, with the Grand Principality of Moscow continuing to grow, and the fragmented Mongol successor states coexisting with the rising Ottoman Empire and the Polish-Lithuanian union.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 1450 AD, displaying a map of Eastern Europe and Central Asia with the Grand Principality of Moscow, the Republic of Novgorod, Poland-Lithuania, and various successor khanates of the Golden Horde, including the Khanate of Kazan, Nogai Horde, and Crimean Khanate. As time progresses to 1478 AD, the Grand Principality of Moscow expands significantly, absorbing territories previously held by the Republic of Novgorod and pushing eastward. By 1480 AD, a dramatic standoff occurs at the Ugra River, marked by a golden banner with crossed swords, symbolizing the confrontation between Moscow and the Khanate of Kazan. The map then transitions to 1505 AD, introducing Vasilii III, depicted in a monochrome portrait wearing a fur-lined hat and regal robes, with his reign noted as 1505–1533. The Grand Principality of Moscow continues to expand, absorbing Smolensk. In 1533 AD, Ivan IV 'The Terrible' appears in a richly detailed portrait, holding a scepter and wearing ornate royal attire, with his reign marked as 1533–1584. The state is renamed 'Russia' in 1547 AD, and by 1558 AD, the map shows the Livonian War (1558–1583) with a banner across the region, as Russia expands westward into territories previously held by the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Kingdom of Sweden. The map also shows the Crimean Khanate and the Nogai Horde still in existence, while the Khanate of Astrakhan and Kazakh Khanate remain in the south and east. By 1565 AD, Ivan IV is shown in a full-body portrait on the right side of the screen, wearing a luxurious fur-lined robe with intricate patterns, emphasizing his imperial authority. The map reflects Russia’s continued territorial growth, with the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth to the west and the Crimean Khanate to the south, illustrating the geopolitical shifts during Ivan IV’s reign.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 1565 AD, displaying a historical map of Eastern Europe with Russia in reddish-brown, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in green, and the Crimean Khanate in blue. segments.caption: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of a grand statue in a wide, open square, likely in Moscow, with Red Square and St. Basil’s Cathedral visible in the distance, establishing a modern Russian setting. The scene transitions to a dark, textured background with the title 'THE ROMANOVS' in bold white letters, overlaid on a faint religious fresco, setting a historical and solemn tone. The narrative then shifts to a detailed animated map of Eastern Europe in 1612, highlighting Russia in reddish-brown, with key cities like Moscow, Novgorod, Smolensk, and Kiev marked. The year changes to 1613, and a historical painting labeled 'ZEMSKY SOBOR' appears, depicting a formal assembly where a man kneels before a seated figure, symbolizing the election of a new tsar. A portrait of Mikhail I (1613–45) in ornate royal regalia with a crown, scepter, and orb appears, signifying his ascension. The map updates to 1618, showing the Treaty of Stolbovo and Truce of Deulino, with documents appearing over the respective regions. In 1645, a portrait of Alexei I (1645–76) replaces Mikhail’s, and by 1649, the 'SOBORNOYE ULOZHENIYE' banner appears with an illustration of three men in traditional attire, one holding a sword, representing the codification of Russian law. The map remains consistent, showing neighboring territories like the Kingdom of Sweden, Poland-Lithuania, and the Crimean Khanate, while the timeline progresses through key events of the Romanov dynasty’s early rule, emphasizing political stability and legal reform.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 1649 with a focus on the Romanov dynasty, displaying portraits of Mikhail I (1613–45) and Alexei I (1645–76) alongside a map of Russia and its neighbors, including the Kingdom of Sweden and the Crimean Khanate. The Sobornoye Ulozheniye, Russia’s legal code, is highlighted with an illustration of peasants working in a field, symbolizing the codification of serfdom. As the timeline advances to 1654, the map expands to show key Russian cities like Novgorod, Moscow, Smolensk, and Kiev, with the Crimean Khanate and Poland-Lithuania to the west and the Ottoman Empire to the south. A full-body portrait of Alexei I appears, emphasizing his imperial authority. The segment then introduces the Old Believers with a black-and-white photograph of a group of bearded men in traditional attire, reflecting religious dissent following the church reforms. The Treaty of Pereyaslav (1654) is marked near Kiev, initiating the Thirteen Years' War (1654–1667) between Russia and Poland-Lithuania, illustrated with arrows moving from Smolensk and Kiev and a painting of Cossacks on horseback. The war’s progression is shown with animated flames engulfing Smolensk and Kiev by 1667, culminating in a map shift to 1670 that reveals further Russian expansion eastward, with new cities like Saratov, Tsaritsyn, and Astrakhan appearing, signaling the empire’s growing reach into the Volga region.

segments.caption: The video segment begins in 1670 with a map of Russia, highlighting key cities such as Moscow, Saratov, Tsaritsyn, and Astrakhan, with animated flames appearing near Saratov and Tsaritsyn, symbolizing unrest. A portrait of Stenka Razin, a Cossack rebel, appears on the right, indicating his role in the uprising. The year shifts to 1671, and a dramatic painting depicts a bearded man in white robes being led by soldiers in red, suggesting a capture or execution, likely of Razin. The map reappears in 1676, showing the reign of Feodor III (1676–82), with the Kingdom of Sweden, Poland-Lithuania, and the Ottoman Empire labeled as neighboring powers. In 1682, a black-and-white illustration shows a nobleman in ornate robes overseeing the punishment of a kneeling figure, with a banner reading 'MESTNICHESTVO ABOLISHED 1682,' marking a significant political reform. The map then displays Princess Sofia (1682–89) as a regent, alongside portraits of Ivan V and Peter I, indicating a power struggle among the Romanovs. By 1686, the map shows the Treaty of Eternal Peace, with a document icon near Kiev, and the Great Turkish War (1683–1699) is noted, reflecting Russia’s expanding conflicts. The visual narrative emphasizes political turmoil, reform, and the rise of new rulers during the late 17th century, with shifting power dynamics and military engagements shaping the Russian state.

Throughout, the map remains textured with a cloudy, aged appearance, and the year counter updates dynamically in the top right corner.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a historical map of Eastern Europe in 1702, highlighting the Kingdom of Sweden, Poland-Lithuania, and Russia, with key cities like Moscow, Smolensk, and Kiev marked. The segment concludes with a title card reading 'THE FOUR EMPRESSES,' transitioning to a new historical phase, with portraits of Peter I, Catherine I (1725–27), and Princess Sofia reappearing, setting the stage for the Romanov dynasty’s female rulers.

The year 1775 is displayed as the segment ends, marking the conclusion of the revolt.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a historical map of Eastern Europe in 1775, showing Russia in reddish-brown, Poland-Lithuania in green, Austria in yellow, and the Ottoman Empire in deep red. The year advances to 1783, and the Crimea Khanate is replaced by the label 'NOVOROSSIYA', indicating Russian annexation. A portrait of Catherine II (Catherine the Great) appears on the left, dressed in an ornate blue and red military-style coat with medals, a white cravat, and a powdered wig, symbolizing her imperial authority. The map then transitions to 1784, with an illustration of the 'PARTITIONS OF POLAND' appearing at the bottom, depicting Catherine, Frederick the Great of Prussia, and Joseph II of Austria dividing a map of Poland. The year shifts to 1795, and the map shows Poland-Lithuania being erased, replaced by Russian, Prussian, and Austrian territories. The scene then moves to 1788, with a new illustration on the left showing two Ottoman figures and a Russian figure on the right, representing the Russo-Turkish War. By 1790, the text 'THE PALE OF SETTLEMENT ESTABLISHED 1791' appears, marking a significant policy under Catherine’s rule. The map remains focused on the Black Sea region, with key cities like Azov, Kerch, and Astrakhan visible, and the Ottoman Empire still present to the south. The overall atmosphere is one of imperial expansion and geopolitical maneuvering, with visual elements emphasizing Catherine’s role in reshaping Eastern Europe.

Two black-and-white photographs appear: one showing a group of exiled men in Siberian winter clothing, and another depicting a long line of prisoners marching through a snowy forest, underscoring the harsh punishment and isolation faced by the rebels. The overall tone is somber and dramatic, using layered visuals and historical imagery to convey the political upheaval and repression of 1825 Russia.

The timeline jumps to 1849, highlighting the 'Hungarian Revolution 1848–49' with a red arrow pointing from Vienna into the Austrian Empire, followed by a portrait of Nicholas I in a red military uniform with medals, labeled 'GENDARME OF EUROPE', emphasizing Russia’s role as a conservative power. In 1851, a sepia photograph of the St. Petersburg–Moscow railway appears, detailing its construction by 50,000 serfs, 395 miles long, averaging 18.5 mph, and carrying 693,000 passengers in its first year. The year 1852 introduces Alexander Herzen (1812–1870), shown with a full beard and dark coat, as a key intellectual figure. Finally, in 1853, the map focuses on the Black Sea and Ottoman Empire, with a banner announcing the 'CRIMEAN WAR 1853–56', marking the beginning of a major conflict. The map’s lighting remains dark and atmospheric, with golden banners and text providing historical context, while the visual narrative flows from political upheaval to technological progress and impending war.

A golden banner appears, announcing the 'CONQUEST OF TURKESTAN 1865–1885,' as the map overlays the modern-day borders of Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan, illustrating the region’s historical significance. The focus then shifts to the geopolitical tension known as 'THE GREAT GAME,' with the map showing British India to the south and Persia to the west, emphasizing the rivalry between Russia and Britain. The scene transitions to 1867, with a new map showing the Pacific Ocean and the Alaska Purchase, marked with the text '$7.2 million = $120 million today' and a question about Alaska’s current worth, estimated at '$4 trillion?'. A photograph of an oil rig in Alaska appears, underscoring its modern economic value. The video then moves to 1869, displaying a portrait of Leo Tolstoy alongside the cover of his novel 'War & Peace,' with a golden banner reading 'WAR & PEACE 1869,' linking literature to the historical context of the era.

segments.caption: The video begins in 1869 with a focus on Russian cultural achievements, prominently featuring a portrait of Leo Tolstoy alongside the cover of his novel 'War & Peace' (СОЧИНЕНИЯ ГРАФА Л. Н. ТОЛСТОГО. ЧАСТЬ ПЯТАЯ. ВОЙНА И МИР.), set against a detailed map of Europe and Asia. A golden banner below reads 'WAR & PEACE 1869'. The scene expands to include portraits of other Russian cultural figures: Tchaikovsky, Dostoyevsky, Mussorgsky, and Turgenev, each labeled with their names on golden banners, emphasizing the flourishing of Russian arts and literature. The timeline then advances to 1877, shifting focus to geopolitical conflict. The map highlights the Russo-Turkish War (1877–78) with animated red arrows indicating military movements from Russia toward the Ottoman Empire, specifically targeting the Danube and regions near the Black Sea. A golden banner announces the war, and a series of historical paintings depict intense battle scenes: soldiers crossing a river under fire, a fierce assault on a mountain pass (Shipka Pass), and a chaotic siege at Plevna. The year changes to 1878, and the map shows the war's progression toward Constantinople, with a red arrow pointing south. A painting of the Congress of Berlin 1878 appears, showing a formal diplomatic gathering of European powers, symbolizing the war's conclusion. The map then updates to reflect the new political landscape, with the emergence of independent states like Romania, Serbia, and Montenegro, marked with dashed borders, while the Ottoman Empire's territory shrinks, illustrating the war's geopolitical consequences.

segments.caption: The video begins with a historical map of Eastern Europe and the Black Sea region in 1878, highlighting the Austro-Hungarian Empire, Ottoman Empire, and key territories like Romania, Serbia, Bulgaria, and Montenegro, with the Black Sea and Constantinople clearly marked. The year 1878 is displayed in the top-right corner. The map then pans to the east, revealing Russia in reddish-brown, with major cities like Moscow, St. Petersburg, and Tobolsk labeled, and the vast expanse of Siberia to the east. A portrait of Alexander II, dressed in ornate military regalia with medals, epaulettes, and a sash, appears on the right side of the screen, symbolizing his role as the 'Liberator' and ruler of Russia. The year changes to 1880, and two black-and-white illustrations appear on the left: one depicting a chaotic street scene with figures in motion, and another showing a violent confrontation with figures falling. The year shifts to 1881, and a vivid, fiery illustration of an explosion appears, followed by a black-and-white engraving of a carriage being attacked, with figures falling and a lamppost in the foreground. The scene transitions to a somber painting of Alexander II lying in state, his face pale and serene, dressed in a red and gold military uniform, resting on a white fur-lined bier, with a red rose beside him. The map reappears, now showing the full expanse of Russia, Siberia, China, and Japan, with the year 1881 still displayed. The explosion illustration reappears, followed by the engraving of the assassination, reinforcing the violent end of Alexander II’s reign. The final frame shows a grand, ornate cathedral with golden domes and intricate architecture, likely the Church of the Savior on Spilled Blood in St. Petersburg, built in memory of the assassinated tsar, symbolizing the enduring legacy of his death.

segments.caption: The video opens with a dramatic, high-contrast exterior shot of the Church of the Savior on Spilled Blood in St. Petersburg, its ornate golden and blue domes standing against a turbulent, cloud-filled sky, with the year '1881' displayed in the top right corner. The scene transitions into a ghostly overlay of the church’s interior, revealing intricate frescoes of saints and a grand, lit chandelier, creating a sacred and somber atmosphere. A portrait of Emperor Alexander II in full military regalia, with a red collar, gold epaulettes, and a sash, fades in on the left, his stern gaze fixed forward. The scene then shifts to a stylized map of Russia, with the emperor now seated on a throne in imperial robes and crown, superimposed over the territory. segments.caption: The video segment opens with a historical map of Europe and Russia in 1917, prominently displaying the year and the word 'RUSSIA' in large, bold letters. The map highlights key cities such as Petrograd, Moscow, Kiev, and Omsk, with national flags of Germany, Austria-Hungary, and the Ottoman Empire marking their territories. Overlaid on the map is a series of black-and-white archival photographs depicting scenes from the Russian Revolution. The first image shows a large crowd of civilians, including women and children, gathered outside a grand building, possibly a government or public institution, with some carrying baskets and bundles. The next image zooms in on a group of women standing near a shopfront with Cyrillic signage, suggesting daily life amidst political upheaval. A subsequent photograph features a group of armed soldiers, many in winter gear, posing together, indicating military involvement in the revolution. Another image captures a massive crowd in a public square, holding a banner with Cyrillic text that reads 'ДА ЗДРАВСТВУЕТ! ВСЕМИРНАЯ СОЦИАЛИСТИЧЕСКАЯ РЕСПУБЛИКА' (Long live the World Socialist Republic!), signaling revolutionary fervor. The sequence transitions to a formal portrait of Tsar Nicholas II seated at a desk, surrounded by military and political figures, symbolizing the old regime. This is followed by a photograph of the Tsar sitting on a tree stump in a forest, labeled 'TSAR'S ABDICATION,' marking the end of his rule. Finally, a portrait of Grand Duke Michael appears, labeled 'GRAND DUKE MICHAEL,' indicating his brief role as the next in line before he declined the throne. The overall atmosphere is one of dramatic political transition, with the visual narrative moving from public unrest to the collapse of imperial authority.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a historical montage set against a stylized map of Europe and Russia in 1917, marked by a prominent '1917' timestamp in the top-right corner. The sequence begins with a black-and-white portrait of Grand Duke Michael in military uniform, labeled with a golden banner, followed by a photograph of Tsar Nicholas II seated on a tree stump in a forest, labeled 'TSAR'S ABDICATION,' symbolizing the collapse of the monarchy. The scene transitions to a formal group photograph of the Provisional Government, with men in suits seated and standing around a table, labeled 'PROVISIONAL GOVERNMENT,' indicating the new political authority. This is followed by a large crowd rally with a banner reading 'ДА ЗДРАВСТВУЕТ МЕЖДУНАРОДНЫЙ ПРАЗДНИК ТРУДА' (Long live the International Labor Day), labeled 'RISE OF THE SOVIETS,' showcasing the growing influence of socialist movements. The next image shows the Petrograd Soviet in session, a massive assembly of workers and soldiers, labeled 'PETROGRAD SOVIET,' emphasizing the power of the soviets. The narrative then shifts to a close-up of Vladimir Lenin addressing a crowd, labeled 'THE BOLSHEVIKS,' highlighting the rise of the radical faction. This is followed by a procession of Bolsheviks marching with banners, reinforcing their organized presence. The final image shows Lenin again, this time with soldiers, labeled 'OCTOBER REVOLUTION,' marking the culmination of the Bolshevik seizure of power. Throughout, the map in the background remains consistent, showing key cities like Petrograd, Moscow, and Kiev, with flags of Germany, Austro-Hungarian Empire, and others, grounding the events in their geopolitical context.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a historical montage centered on the October Revolution of 1917, set against a stylized map of Europe highlighting Russia, Germany, and the Austro-Hungarian Empire. A black-and-white photograph of Vladimir Lenin, dressed in a military-style coat and cap, is shown addressing a group of soldiers, with the text 'OCTOBER REVOLUTION' displayed on a golden banner below. The scene transitions to a wide shot of a grand, snow-covered building, likely the Winter Palace, with horse-drawn carriages and figures moving in the foreground, reinforcing the revolutionary setting. The next sequence features a dramatic black-and-white image of Lenin standing atop a wooden platform, passionately addressing a massive crowd in a public square, with soldiers and civilians gathered around, some holding flags. The atmosphere is charged with revolutionary fervor, and the crowd’s attention is fixed on Lenin. The scene then shifts to a four-panel split screen showing military imagery: cavalry on horseback, infantry marching with rifles, a chaotic battlefield, and a tank, all in black-and-white, symbolizing the military forces involved in the revolution. The segment concludes with black screens displaying credits, including the narrator Charles Nove, music by Johnny De'Ath, and a list of Patreon supporters, associate producers, and special thanks, followed by the final credit: 'Written, Animated & Produced by Toby Groom.' The overall tone is historical and dramatic, emphasizing the pivotal moment of the October Revolution through archival-style imagery and a structured narrative progression.
